title: Entity-Relationship Diagrams
summary: ER modeling: entities, attributes, relationships, cardinality, and converting ER diagrams to relational tables.
content_html:
Entity-Relationship (ER) Model

ER diagrams visually represent the logical structure of a database. Used in the design phase before implementation.

Core Components

  • Entity: A real-world object or concept (rectangle). E.g., Student, Course.
  • Attribute: Property of an entity (oval). E.g., StudentID, Name, Age.
  • Relationship: Association between entities (diamond). E.g., "enrolls in".

Types of Attributes

  • Simple: Atomic, not divisible (Age, ID).
  • Composite: Made of sub-attributes (FullName → FirstName + LastName).
  • Multi-valued: Can have multiple values (PhoneNumbers). Shown with double oval.
  • Derived: Computed from other attributes (Age from DateOfBirth). Shown with dashed oval.
  • Key attribute: Uniquely identifies entity. Underlined in diagram.

Cardinality (Relationship Types)

  • One-to-One (1:1): Each entity in A relates to at most one entity in B. E.g., Person—Passport.
  • One-to-Many (1:N): One entity in A relates to many in B. E.g., Department—Employees.
  • Many-to-Many (M:N): Many in A relate to many in B. E.g., Students—Courses.

Converting ER to Relational Tables

1. Each entity → one table
2. Each attribute → one column
3. Key attribute → primary key

1:N relationship:
  Add FK in the "many" side table pointing to "one" side PK.
  
M:N relationship:
  Create a junction/bridge table with FKs from both entities.
  
Example: Students—Courses (M:N)
  Enrollment(StudentID FK, CourseID FK, EnrollDate)
  (PK = composite of StudentID + CourseID)

Participation Constraints

  • Total participation (double line): Every entity MUST participate in the relationship.
  • Partial participation (single line): Entity participation is optional.

Weak Entities

A weak entity cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone — it depends on a related (owner) entity. Shown with a double rectangle in the ER diagram.
